title: 09_shuffled_time_series.ipynb
descriptionMd:
## Mission: Make the Signal Desk's Backtest Honest

The signal-research desk trains a gradient-boosted classifier on six years of daily SPY features — lagged returns, rolling volatility, a 14-day RSI — to predict whether the index closes higher over the next five sessions. The promotion gate computes two numbers: a 5-fold cross-validated backtest AUC, and a live-replay AUC that trains on the first 80% of history and scores the untouched final 20%, exactly how the model would run in production.

The gate is red in the worst possible way. The backtest reports a stellar 0.913; the live replay reports a coin-flip 0.521; and the parity assert comparing them has blown up. The features, the model, and the data are identical for both numbers — the only difference is how rows were assigned to train and validation. Work out why the backtest believes in a model that the future has already refuted.
initialCode:
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.ensemble import GradientBoostingClassifier
from sklearn.metrics import roc_auc_score
from sklearn.model_selection import KFold, cross_val_score

df = pd.read_csv("spy_daily_features.csv", parse_dates=["date"])
df = df.sort_values("date").reset_index(drop=True)
X = df[["ret_1d", "ret_5d", "vol_10d", "rsi_14"]]
y = df["up_next_5d"]

cv = KFold(n_splits=5, shuffle=True, random_state=42)
model = GradientBoostingClassifier(random_state=42)
backtest_auc = cross_val_score(model, X, y, cv=cv, scoring="roc_auc").mean()
print(f"backtest AUC: {backtest_auc:.3f}")

cutoff = int(len(df) * 0.8)
model.fit(X.iloc[:cutoff], y.iloc[:cutoff])
live_probs = model.predict_proba(X.iloc[cutoff:])[:, 1]
live_auc = roc_auc_score(y.iloc[cutoff:], live_probs)
print(f"live-replay AUC: {live_auc:.3f}")
assert abs(backtest_auc - live_auc) < 0.05, (
    f"backtest/live divergence: AUC {backtest_auc:.3f} in CV vs {live_auc:.3f} forward"
)
print("backtest/live parity confirmed")
traceback:
backtest AUC: 0.913

options.label: Turn off shuffling — KFold(n_splits=5) so the folds are contiguous blocks of days
options.patchCode:
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.ensemble import GradientBoostingClassifier
from sklearn.metrics import roc_auc_score
from sklearn.model_selection import KFold, cross_val_score

options.rationale: Contiguous folds remove the worst of the neighbor-memorization, which is why the number falls from 0.913 — but four of the five folds still train on blocks of days that come *after* the block being validated, and the 5-session forward label still bleeds across every fold boundary. The model keeps a diluted preview of the future, the backtest stays optimistic at 0.594, and the parity gate stays red. Unshuffled K-fold is unordered validation with neater seams, not time-ordered validation.

options.label: Replace KFold with TimeSeriesSplit so every fold validates strictly after its training window
options.patchCode:
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.ensemble import GradientBoostingClassifier
from sklearn.metrics import roc_auc_score
from sklearn.model_selection import TimeSeriesSplit, cross_val_score

df = pd.read_csv("spy_daily_features.csv", parse_dates=["date"])
df = df.sort_values("date").reset_index(drop=True)
X = df[["ret_1d", "ret_5d", "vol_10d", "rsi_14"]]
y = df["up_next_5d"]

cv = TimeSeriesSplit(n_splits=5)
model = GradientBoostingClassifier(random_state=42)
backtest_auc = cross_val_score(model, X, y, cv=cv, scoring="roc_auc").mean()
print(f"backtest AUC: {backtest_auc:.3f}")

cutoff = int(len(df) * 0.8)
model.fit(X.iloc[:cutoff], y.iloc[:cutoff])
live_probs = model.predict_proba(X.iloc[cutoff:])[:, 1]
live_auc = roc_auc_score(y.iloc[cutoff:], live_probs)
print(f"live-replay AUC: {live_auc:.3f}")
assert abs(backtest_auc - live_auc) < 0.05, (
    f"backtest/live divergence: AUC {backtest_auc:.3f} in CV vs {live_auc:.3f} forward"
)
print("backtest/live parity confirmed")
options.resultLog:
backtest AUC: 0.528
live-replay AUC: 0.521
backtest/live parity confirmed
options.rationale: TimeSeriesSplit forward-chains: fold k trains on the first k blocks of history and validates on the block that immediately follows, so every validation row is strictly later than every training row — the same temporal contract the deployed model lives under. The backtest estimate collapses to 0.528 and agrees with the 0.521 live replay within tolerance. The number is humbler, but it is the first one in this notebook that means anything.

options.label: Relax the parity tolerance from 0.05 to 0.45 — markets are noisy and the two splits measure different things
options.patchCode:
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.ensemble import GradientBoostingClassifier
from sklearn.metrics import roc_auc_score
from sklearn.model_selection import KFold, cross_val_score

df = pd.read_csv("spy_daily_features.csv", parse_dates=["date"])
df = df.sort_values("date").reset_index(drop=True)
X = df[["ret_1d", "ret_5d", "vol_10d", "rsi_14"]]
y = df["up_next_5d"]

cv = KFold(n_splits=5, shuffle=True, random_state=42)
model = GradientBoostingClassifier(random_state=42)
backtest_auc = cross_val_score(model, X, y, cv=cv, scoring="roc_auc").mean()
print(f"backtest AUC: {backtest_auc:.3f}")

cutoff = int(len(df) * 0.8)
model.fit(X.iloc[:cutoff], y.iloc[:cutoff])
live_probs = model.predict_proba(X.iloc[cutoff:])[:, 1]
live_auc = roc_auc_score(y.iloc[cutoff:], live_probs)
print(f"live-replay AUC: {live_auc:.3f}")
assert abs(backtest_auc - live_auc) < 0.45, (
    f"backtest/live divergence: AUC {backtest_auc:.3f} in CV vs {live_auc:.3f} forward"
)
print("backtest/live parity confirmed")
options.resultLog:
backtest AUC: 0.913
live-replay AUC: 0.521
backtest/live parity confirmed
options.rationale: Green, and a lie. The 0.392 gap is not market noise — the live replay already demonstrated the model has no edge on unseen futures, and the parity assert existed precisely to stop a 0.91 fantasy from reaching a slide deck. Widening a tolerance until a failing invariant passes does not repair the methodology; it deletes the alarm while the fire keeps burning, and the 0.913 now ships with the gate's apparent blessing.

hints: Cross-validation's core assumption is that rows are interchangeable — any row may train while any other validates. Daily market rows are not interchangeable: each one carries information about its calendar neighbors, and the model's whole job is to predict forward. Ask what 'training data' means when the rows have an arrow of time.

Line 11: `KFold(shuffle=True)` deals six years of trading days into folds at random, so nearly every validation day has its immediate neighbors — and days from after it — sitting in the training set. scikit-learn ships a splitter built for exactly this ordering constraint.
explanationMd:
### Why the bug occurs
`KFold(shuffle=True)` assigns trading days to folds at random, which destroys the arrow of time twice over. First, every validation day's calendar neighbors land in training — and with a label defined over the next five sessions, adjacent days share four of the five sessions that determine their labels, so the model can score a day by recalling its near-twins rather than through any real signal. Second, most of each training fold occurs *after* the days being validated, so the model is fit on regimes and volatility clusters that had not happened yet. Both effects inflate the CV AUC to 0.913. The live replay — train on the past, score the future — is immune to both, returns 0.521, and the parity assert exposes the gap.

### Why the fix is correct
`TimeSeriesSplit(n_splits=5)` forward-chains: each fold trains on an initial segment of history and validates on the block that immediately follows, so every validation row is strictly later than every training row. That reproduces the deployed model's temporal contract inside cross-validation, which is the entire point of a backtest. The estimate drops to 0.528 and matches the live replay within tolerance — not because the model got better, but because the measurement finally got honest. The general rule: a validation split must give the model exactly the information set it will have at decision time, and for ordered data that means the future is never in the training fold.
recruiterReview: Correct call, and you resisted both traps. The principle is that a validation scheme must reproduce the information set the model will have at decision time — for ordered data that means train strictly on the past, score strictly on the future, which is exactly what forward chaining gives you. I'm especially glad you didn't take option C: an engineer who widens a tolerance to silence a parity check is an engineer who ships 0.91 slide decks for 0.52 models. One improvement before this lands in the research pipeline: with a 5-session forward label, add a gap between train and validation (TimeSeriesSplit's gap parameter, or a purge/embargo) so the label horizon can't straddle the fold boundary — right now the last few training labels still peek into the validation window. Approved. ✅

tutorial.bodyMd:
## Validating Models When the Data Has an Arrow of Time

K-fold cross-validation rests on an assumption so foundational it is rarely said out loud: the rows are exchangeable. Shuffle them, deal them into folds, and every fold is a fair sample of the same distribution. Snapshots of independent customers mostly satisfy that. Time series never do. A day on a market, a sensor, or a sales ledger is correlated with the days around it — and, more fatally, the entire purpose of the model is to predict *forward*. The validation scheme has to respect that, or the score it produces describes a universe the deployed model will never inhabit.

### Two distinct leaks from one shuffle
Shuffled K-fold on ordered data leaks in two compounding ways. **Neighbor leakage**: rolling features and horizon labels overlap across adjacent rows. If the label is "return over the next 5 days," Monday and Tuesday share four of the five days that define their labels; train on one, validate on the other, and the model is grading itself on near-duplicates. **Future leakage**: with random fold assignment, most of each training set postdates the validation rows, so the model fits on regimes, level shifts, and volatility clusters that had not happened yet — knowledge no production model can have.

### Forward chaining
The honest scheme is forward chaining, implemented in sklearn as `TimeSeriesSplit`: fold 1 trains on the first block and validates on the second; fold 2 trains on blocks 1–2 and validates on block 3; and so on. Every validation row is strictly later than every training row in its fold. Notice the costs and accept them: early folds train on little data, the earliest block never gets scored, and fold scores are not independent of each other. Those are prices of honesty, not flaws to engineer away.

### Purging and embargoes
Strict ordering is necessary but not always sufficient. If your label looks h steps ahead, the last h training rows before each boundary have labels computed from validation-period values. Purge them — drop those boundary rows, or pass `gap=h` to TimeSeriesSplit. For heavily autocorrelated features, walk-forward schemes in finance add an embargo as well: a buffer after the validation window before any data is reused for training. Purged, embargoed CV is table stakes in financial ML for exactly this reason.

### Keep a parity alarm
The cheapest structural defense is the one this notebook already had: alongside any CV number, maintain one plain forward holdout — train on the first 80%, score the rest — and assert the two agree. Leakage almost always inflates CV far above the forward replay, so the pair works as a smoke detector for any splitter mistake anyone introduces later. A backtest you cannot reconcile with a simulated deployment is not a measurement; it is a wish.

### Common variations of this bug
- `train_test_split` with its default `shuffle=True` on ordered logs — the same mistake with one split instead of five.
- Random CV with rolling-window features (moving averages, lags), so feature windows straddle the train/validation boundary.
- Overlapping forward-return or churn-horizon labels shared between train-set and validation-set neighbors.
- Hyperparameter tuning with shuffled CV followed by an honest forward test — the chosen params themselves absorbed the future.
- Scaling or imputing with statistics computed over the full history before splitting, leaking future means into the past.
- Grouped data (users, patients, stores) split row-wise so the same entity appears in train and validation — the i.i.d. cousin of this bug.
